{"action":"opened","number":56473,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56473","id":2165711685,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM6BFiNF","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56473","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56473.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56473.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56473","number":56473,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"WIP: Batch Channel Operations","user":{"login":"mrufsvold","id":86363075,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MzYzMDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/86363075?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold","html_url":"https://github.com/mrufsvold","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Add `append!(ch, iter)` and `take(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1831504442","pull_request_review_id":2419087972,"id":1831504442,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5tKoo6","diff_hunk":"@@ -553,6 +664,127 @@ function take_unbuffered(c::Channel{T}) where T
     end
 end
 
+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+[1,2,3]
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer) where {T}
+    return _take(c, n, Vector{T}(undef, n))
+end
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector{T2}) where {T2,T<:T2}","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"3e7000407ea6bcda6ebc00d2879374616c6b7295","original_commit_id":"3e7000407ea6bcda6ebc00d2879374616c6b7295","user":{"login":"mrufsvold","id":86363075,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MzYzMDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/86363075?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold","html_url":"https://github.com/mrufsvold","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"I am limiting the buffer to `AbstractVector` because, if the channel closes, we need to return fewer than `n` elements. In that case, we need to resize the buffer. So that precludes `Memory` or `Matrix` as buffer types. I'm not sure if there is a more general way to accept more buffer types here. `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation

julia> GC.gc()

Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+[1,2,3]
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer) where {T}
+    return _take(c, n, Vector{T}(undef, n))
+end
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector{T2}) where {T2,T<:T2}
+    # buffer is user defined, so make sure it has the correct size
+    if length(buffer) != n
+        resize!(buffer, n)
+    end
+    return _take(c, n, buffer)
+end
+
+function _take(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector) where {T}
+    buffered = isbuffered(c)
+    # short-circuit for small n
+    if n == 0
+        return buffer
+    elseif n == 1
+        try
+            x = take!(c)
+            @inbounds buffer[begin] = x
+        catch e
+            # when provided a batch size, take should not error on a closed column
+            # so we need to return an empty buffer if we fail to get one element.
+            if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                empty!(buffer)
+                return buffer
+            else
+                rethrow()
+            end
+        end
+        return buffer
+    end
+    buffered ? take_buffered(c, n, buffer) : take_unbuffered(c, buffer)
+end
+
+function take_buffered(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector) where {T}
+    elements_taken = 0 # number of elements taken so far
+    idx1 = firstindex(buffer)
+    target_buffer_len = min(n, c.sz_max)
+    lock(c)
+    try
+        while elements_taken < n && (isopen(c) || isready(c))
+            # wait until the channel has at least min_n elements or is full
+            while length(c.data) < target_buffer_len && isopen(c)
+                try
+                    wait(c.cond_take)
+                catch e
+                    if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                        break
+                    else
+                        rethrow()
+                    end
+                end
+            end
+            # take as many elements as possible from the buffer
+            n_to_take = min(n - elements_taken, length(c.data))
+            idx_start = idx1 + elements_taken
+            idx_end = idx_start + n_to_take - 1
+            for (res_i, data_i) in Iterators.zip(idx_start:idx_end, eachindex(c.data))
+                @inbounds buffer[res_i] = c.data[data_i]
+            end
+            deleteat!(c.data, 1:n_to_take)
+            elements_taken += n_to_take
+            _increment_n_avail(c, -n_to_take)
+            foreach(_ -> notify(c.cond_put, nothing, true, false), `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel{T}, vec::AbstractArray) where {T}
+    # shortcircuit for small vecs
+    if length(vec) == 0
+        return c
+    elseif length(vec) == 1
+        put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+        return c
+    end
+    isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, vec) : append_unbuffered(c, vec)
+end
+
+function append!(c1::Channel, c2::Channel{T}) where {T}
+    buff_len = max(c1.sz_max, c2.sz_max)
+    if buff_len == 0
+        return append_unbuffered(c1, c2)
+    end

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 mean   4.724 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 max    15.609 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

 median 1.889 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)
 mean   2.168 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 mean   4.724 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 max    15.609 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

 mean   2.168 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)
 max    7.967 ms (128 allocs: 780.312 KiB)

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel{T}, vec::AbstractArray) where {T}
+    # shortcircuit for small vecs
+    if length(vec) == 0
+        return c
+    elseif length(vec) == 1
+        put!(c, @inbounds `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1831756622","pull_request_review_id":2419497930,"id":1831756622,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5tLmNO","diff_hunk":"@@ -454,6 +454,118 @@ function put_unbuffered(c::Channel, v)
     return v
 end
 
+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c)
+1
+
+julia> take!(c)
+2
+
+julia> take!(c)
+3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel, iter::T) where {T}
+    if hasmethod(length, Tuple{T})
+        len = length(iter)
+        # shortcircuit for small short objects
+        if len == 0
+            return c
+        elseif len == 1
+            put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+            return c
+        end
+    end
+
+    return isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, iter) : append_unbuffered(c, iter)
+end
+function append!(c1::Channel, c2::Channel{T}) where {T}
+    buff_len = max(c1.sz_max, c2.sz_max)
+    if buff_len == 0
+        return append_unbuffered(c1, c2)
+    end
+
+    buff = Vector{T}(undef, buff_len)
+    while isopen(c2) || isready(c2)
+        take!(c2, buff_len, buff)
+        append_buffered(c1, buff)
+    end
+    return c1
+end
+
+function append_unbuffered(c::Channel, iter)
+    for v in iter
+        put!(c, v)
+    end
+    return c
+end
+
+function append_buffered(c::Channel{T}, iter::I) where {T,I}","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"8203cd857314b0605a7b1b01460f5c1dbacdd048","original_commit_id":"8203cd857314b0605a7b1b01460f5c1dbacdd048","user":{"login":"mrufsvold","id":86363075,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MzYzMDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/86363075?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold","html_url":"https://github.com/mrufsvold","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"I am not clear on how to deal with arbitrary types while relying on `Base.IteratorSize`. For example

```julia
julia> using DataFrames

julia> df = DataFrame()
0×0 DataFrame

julia> Base.IteratorSize(df)
Base.HasLength()

julia> length(df)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching length(::DataFrame)
The function `length` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types.       
You may need to implement the `length` method or define `IteratorSize` for this type to be `SizeUnknown`.

Closest candidates are:
  length(::Base.AsyncGenerator)
   @ Base asyncmap.jl:392
  length(::CompositeException)
   @ Base task.jl:51
  length(::Markdown.MD)
   @ Markdown C:\Users\mrufsvold\.julia\juliaup\julia-nightly\share\julia\stdlib\v1.12\Markdown\src\parse\parse.jl:37
  ...

Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[32]:1
```
So getting `Base.HasLength()` back does not imply that you can safely query `length(x)`. Inversely:

```julia
julia> m = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> Base.IteratorSize(m)
Base.HasShape{2}()

julia> length(m)
4

Operations","user":{"login":"mrufsvold","id":86363075,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MzYzMDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/86363075?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold","html_url":"https://github.com/mrufsvold","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Add `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+3-element Vector{Int64}:
+ 1
+ 2
+ 3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer) where {T}
+    return _take(c, n, Vector{T}(undef, n))
+end
+function take!(c::Channel, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector)
+    # buffer is user defined, so make sure it has the correct size
+    if length(buffer) != n
+        resize!(buffer, n)
+ `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+3-element Vector{Int64}:
+ 1
+ 2
+ 3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector = Vector{T}()) where {T}
+    # for small-ish n, we can avoid resizing the buffer as we push elements
+    if n < 2^16","path":"base/channels.jl","commit_id":"f71759483377720ac64573e731a6547a84dc73cc","original_commit_id":"f71759483377720ac64573e731a6547a84dc73cc","user":{"login":"mrufsvold","id":86363075,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MzYzMDc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/86363075?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold","html_url":"https://github.com/mrufsvold","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mrufsvold/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This is fairly arbitrary. `append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c)
+1
+
+julia> take!(c)
+2
+
+julia> take!(c)
+3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel, iter::T) where {T}
+    if hasmethod(length, Tuple{T})
+        len = length(iter)
+        # shortcircuit for small short objects
+        if len == 0
+            return c
+        elseif len == 1
+            put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+            return c
+        end
+    end
+
+    return isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, iter) : append_unbuffered(c, iter)
+end

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

+"""
+    append!(c::Channel, iter)
+
+Append all items in `iter` to the channel `c`. If the channel is buffered, this operation requires
+fewer `lock` operations than individual `put!`s. Blocks if the channel is full.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c)
+1
+
+julia> take!(c)
+2
+
+julia> take!(c)
+3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function append!(c::Channel, iter::T) where {T}
+    if hasmethod(length, Tuple{T})
+        len = length(iter)
+        # shortcircuit for small short objects
+        if len == 0
+            return c
+        elseif len == 1
+            put!(c, @inbounds vec[begin])
+            return c
+        end
+    end
+
+    return isbuffered(c) ? append_buffered(c, iter) : append_unbuffered(c, iter)
+end
+function append!(c1::Channel, c2::Channel{T}) where {T}
+    # if the source channel has no buffer or only one slot, there is no benefit to grouping the lock
+    if min(c1.sz_max, c2.sz_max) < 2
+        return isbuffered(c1) ? append_buffered(c1, c2) : append_unbuffered(c1, c2)
+    end
+    i = 0
+    lock(c2)
+    try
+        while isopen(c2) || isready(c2)
+            # wait for data to become available
+            while isempty(c2.data)
+                try
+                    check_channel_state(c2)
+                    wait(c2.cond_take)
+                catch e
+                    if isa(e, InvalidStateException) && e.state === :closed
+                        break
+                    else
+                        rethrow()
+                    end
+                end
+            end
+
+            lock(c1)
+            try
+                while length(c1.data) == c1.sz_max
+                    check_channel_state(c1)
+                    wait(c1.cond_put)
+                end
+
+                # append as many as possible to c1
+                n = min(c1.sz_max - length(c1.data), length(c2.data))
+                _increment_n_avail(c1, n)
+                c1_len_og = length(c1.data)
+
+                resize!(c1.data, c1_len_og + n)
+                copyto!(c1.data, c1_len_og + 1, c2.data, 1, n)
+                deleteat!(c2.data, 1:n)
+
+                _increment_n_avail(c2, -n)
+                notify(c1.cond_take, nothing, true, false)
+                foreach(_ -> notify(c2.cond_put, nothing, true, false), 1:n)
+            finally
+                unlock(c1)
+            end
+        end
+    finally
+        unlock(c2)
+    end
+    return c1
+end
+
+function append_unbuffered(c::Channel, iter)
+    for v in iter
+        put!(c, v)
+    end
+    return c
+end
+
+function append_buffered(c::Channel{T}, iter::I) where {T,I}
+    converted_items = Iterators.Stateful(Iterators.map(x -> convert(T, x), iter))

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+3-element Vector{Int64}:
+ 1
+ 2
+ 3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector = Vector{T}()) where {T}
+    # for small-ish n, we can avoid resizing the buffer as we push elements
+    if n < 2^16
+        resize!(buffer, n)
+    end
+
+    buffered = isbuffered(c)
+    # short-circuit for small n
+    if n == 0
+        return buffer
+    elseif n == 1
+        try
+            x = take!(c)
+            buffer[begin] = x
+        catch e
+            # when provided a batch size, take should not error on a closed channel
+            # so we need to return an empty buffer if we fail to get one element.
+            if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                empty!(buffer)
+            else
+                rethrow()
+            end
+        end
+        return buffer
+    end
+    return buffered ? take_buffered(c, n, buffer) : take_unbuffered(c, n, buffer)
+end
+
+function take_buffered(c::Channel{T}, n, buffer::AbstractVector) where {T}
+    elements_taken = 0 # number of elements taken so far
+    idx1 = firstindex(buffer)
+    target_buffer_len = min(n, c.sz_max)
+    lock(c)
+    try
+        while elements_taken < n && (isopen(c) || isready(c))
+            # wait until the channel has at least min_n elements or is full
+            while length(c.data) < target_buffer_len && isopen(c)
+                try
+                    wait(c.cond_take)
+                catch e
+                    if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                        break
+                    else
+                        rethrow()
+                    end
+                end
+            end
+            # take as many elements as possible from the buffer
+            n_to_take = min(n - elements_taken, length(c.data))
+            idx_start = idx1 + elements_taken
+            idx_end = idx_start + n_to_take - 1
+            if idx_end > lastindex(buffer)
+                resize!(buffer, idx_end)
+            end
+            # since idx_start/end are both created relative to `firstindex(buffer)`, they are safe to use

`append!(ch, iter)` and `take!(ch, n)` functions that operate on channels in batches. When using a buffered channel, this significantly reduces the overhead of acquiring the lock of reach `put!/take!`.

Here is some benchmarking code:

```julia
function bench_basic(item_n, buffer_len)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            for j in items
                put!(ch, j)
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                x = take!(ch)
                res[offset+j] = x
            end
        end
    end
    res
end


function bench_batch(item_n, buffer_len, batch_size=buffer_len > 0 ? buffer_len : 100)
    items = collect(1:item_n)
    ch = Channel{Int}(buffer_len)
    task_n = Threads.nthreads()
    res = Vector{Int}(undef, item_n * task_n)

    for _ in 1:task_n
        Threads.@spawn begin
            i = 1
            while i <= item_n
                chunk = @view items[i:min(i + batch_size - 1, item_n)]
                append!(ch, chunk)
                i += batch_size
            end
        end
    end

    @sync for i in Base.OneTo(task_n)
        Threads.@spawn let offset = (i - 1) * item_n
            buff = Vector{Int}(undef, batch_size)
            batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
            batch_len = length(batch)
            batch_i = 1
            for j in Base.OneTo(item_n)
                if batch_i > batch_len
                    batch = take!(ch, batch_size, buff)
                    batch_i = 1
                    batch_len = length(batch)
                end
                x = batch[batch_i]
                res[offset+j] = x
                batch_i += 1
            end
        end

    end
    res
end
```

Here are results at different buffer sizes:

```julia
julia> using Chairmarks

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        129.346 ms (76015 allocs: 1.851 MiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 0)
Benchmark: 1 sample with 1 evaluation
        128.819 ms (76031 allocs: 1.858 MiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        71.047 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)
        110.748 ms (104 allocs: 709.797 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 10)
Benchmark: 4 samples with 1 evaluation
        23.534 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        24.859 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        25.958 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)
        29.972 ms (119 allocs: 710.703 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 3 samples with 1 evaluation
        34.429 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.284 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)
        37.844 ms (107 allocs: 716.844 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 100)
Benchmark: 21 samples with 1 evaluation
 min    3.267 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 median 3.525 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 mean   4.724 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)
 max    15.609 ms (119 allocs: 717.594 KiB)

julia> GC.gc()

julia> @be bench_basic(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 2 samples with 1 evaluation
        49.205 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)
        113.279 ms (111 allocs: 763.156 KiB)

julia> @be bench_batch(10000, 1000)
Benchmark: 44 samples with 1 evaluation

+"""
+    take!(c::Channel, n::Integer[, buffer::AbstractVector])
+
+Take at most `n` items from the [`Channel`](@ref) `c` and return them in a `Vector`.
+If `buffer` is provided, it will be used to store the result. If the channel closes before
+`n` items are taken, the result will contain only the items that were available. For
+buffered channels, this operation requires fewer `lock` operations than individual `take!(c)`s.
+
+# Examples
+
+```jldoctest
+julia> c = Channel(3);
+
+julia> append!(c, 1:3);
+
+julia> take!(c, 3, zeros(Int, 3))
+3-element Vector{Int64}:
+ 1
+ 2
+ 3
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires at least Julia 1.12.
+"""
+function take!(c::Channel{T}, n::Integer, buffer::AbstractVector = Vector{T}()) where {T}
+    # for small-ish n, we can avoid resizing the buffer as we push elements
+    if n < 2^16
+        resize!(buffer, n)
+    end
+
+    buffered = isbuffered(c)
+    # short-circuit for small n
+    if n == 0
+        return buffer
+    elseif n == 1
+        try
+            x = take!(c)
+            buffer[begin] = x
+        catch e
+            # when provided a batch size, take should not error on a closed channel
+            # so we need to return an empty buffer if we fail to get one element.
+            if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                empty!(buffer)
+            else
+                rethrow()
+            end
+        end
+        return buffer
+    end
+    return buffered ? take_buffered(c, n, buffer) : take_unbuffered(c, n, buffer)
+end
+
+function take_buffered(c::Channel{T}, n, buffer::AbstractVector) where {T}
+    elements_taken = 0 # number of elements taken so far
+    idx1 = firstindex(buffer)
+    target_buffer_len = min(n, c.sz_max)
+    lock(c)
+    try
+        while elements_taken < n && (isopen(c) || isready(c))
+            # wait until the channel has at least min_n elements or is full
+            while length(c.data) < target_buffer_len && isopen(c)
+                try
+                    wait(c.cond_take)
+                catch e
+                    if e isa InvalidStateException && e.state === :closed
+                        break
+                    else
+                        rethrow()
+                    end
+                end
+            end
+            # take as many elements as possible from the buffer
+            n_to_take = min(n - elements_taken, length(c.data))
+            idx_start = idx1 + elements_taken
+            idx_end = idx_start + n_to_take - 1
+            if idx_end > lastindex(buffer)
+                resize!(buffer, idx_end)
+            end
+            # since idx_start/end are both created relative to `firstindex(buffer)`, they are safe to use
+            # them as indices for the buffer
+            @views copy!(buffer[idx_start:idx_end], c.data[1:n_to_take])
+            deleteat!(c.data, 1:n_to_take)
+            _increment_n_avail(c, -n_to_take)
+            foreach(_ -> notify(c.cond_put, nothing, true, false), 1:n_to_take)
+            elements_taken += n_to_take
+        end
+
+        # if we broke early, we need to remove the extra slots from the result
+        if elements_taken < length(buffer)
+            deleteat!(buffer, firstindex(buffer)+elements_taken:lastindex(buffer))
+        end
+        return buffer
+    finally
+        unlock(c)
+    end
+end
+

+    idx1 = firstindex(buffer)
+    last_idx = idx1 - 1
+    lock(c)
+    try
+        for i in idx1:(idx1+n-1)
+            try
+                if i <= lastindex(buffer)
+                    buffer[i] = take_unbuffered(c)
+                else
+                    push!(buffer,
